title: Yanagisawa A-50 — diagnosing the G# leak
summary: Used the LED leak light to confirm the G# cup pad isn't seating. Resonator visibly off-center on the low A pad. Both need replacing.
body:
First proper leak test session. Set up the LED light inside the bore and worked through the keys one by one in a dark room. The G# cluster lit up immediately — the pad cup is closing, but the pad itself has hardened and lost its impression of the tone hole rim. The low A was subtler: the resonator has shifted maybe 1mm off-center, just enough that one side of the pad lifts when the key closes.

Plan: replace both pads with Pisoni Pro pads, reseat resonators with shellac. Ordered 2mm and 3mm cork sheet for the G# key height adjustment.

Update 2026-04-17: bench photos of the horn, the G# cluster, the bell engraving and mouthpiece for reference.

steps.description: LED leak light test in dark room. G# cluster lit up immediately — hardened pad lost its impression of the tone hole rim.
Low A resonator shifted ~1mm off-center, causing one side of the pad to lift on close. Both pads need replacing.
Ordered Pisoni Pro pads, shellac for resonator reseating, 2mm and 3mm cork sheet for G# key height adjustment.
Full-length and close-up reference shots of the G# cluster, low-B/Bb guard, bell engraving and bow — baseline before pad work begins.
Detail of the lower stack and bell bow. Used to map pad positions before ordering the final resonator set.

title: Couesnon Monopole Conservatoire II — detached post to solder
summary: A key post on the bell section has come loose from the body. Identified, cleaned the contact faces, ready to re-solder before moving on to tuning.
body:
Vintage French alto, bare/lacquered brass with the characteristic Couesnon pierced key guards and the trident-style bell brace. The horn came in with a post lifted clean off the body — one of the stack posts near the bell bow — so the connected rod can't hold its pivot and the whole cluster flops.

Today's work: laid the horn out on the bench with the separated neck, matched the post to its footprint on the body, cleaned both surfaces back to bright brass with a fine scraper and isopropyl, and dry-fit to confirm alignment. Next bench session: tin both faces, soft-solder with a low-temp silver-bearing alloy (trying to avoid lifting neighbouring lacquer), then dress and polish.

After the post is back on, the real work starts: full tuning pass — regulation, pad seating check on every cup, spring tensions, cork thicknesses. More entries to come.

steps.description: Horn on the bench with neck and mouthpiece laid out. Trident bell brace and pierced guards intact — typical Monopole Conservatoire II detailing.
Neck cork inspected — compressed but still serviceable for now. Will revisit after the post is resoldered and the horn is back together for a proper leak pass.
Detached post located on the bell section. Dry-fit confirms the footprint matches — no bent rod, nothing sheared beyond the solder joint itself.
Cleaned both mating faces back to bright brass. Next bench session: tin both surfaces and soft-solder with a low-temp silver-bearing alloy to avoid lifting the adjacent lacquer.
After the post holds: full regulation and tuning pass — pad seating on every cup, spring tensions, cork thicknesses, intonation check up the horn. More entries to come.
